entries.statement: ∀ p : AtomicResidualState, ∀ R : Finset AtomicResidualState, (∀ q : AtomicResidualState, q ∈ R ↔ Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep p q) → let F : Set AtomicResidualState → Set AtomicResidualState := fun S => {q : AtomicResidualState | q ∈ R ∧ (q = ([# (p.2)], p.2) ∨ ∃ r : AtomicResidualState, AtomicResidualGraphStep q r ∧ r ∈ S)}; let A : Set AtomicResidualState := {q : AtomicResidualState | q ∈ R ∧ AtomicResidualGraphAccepts q}; A ⊆ (↑R : Set AtomicResidualState) ∧ F A = A ∧ Nat.iterate F R.card (∅ : Set AtomicResidualState) = A ∧ ∀ S : Set AtomicResidualState, S ⊆ (↑R : Set AtomicResidualState) → F S ⊆ S → A ⊆ S
entries.rationale: This is the strongest summary-level theorem suggested by the verified core: acceptance is already characterized semantically on the exact reachable DAG, and this candidate turns that semantic characterization into a canonical, order-independent finite computation. It reorganizes the current theory around one operator-level statement instead of separate finiteness, reachability, least-subset, and sweep facts.
entries.reason: The current candidate does not clear the paper-unit bar because the available direct-reading anchors already place it very near known Lambek decision-procedure and spurious-ambiguity-elimination territory, while the claimed delta reads as a consolidation of existing reachability, least-subset, and sweep facts rather than a genuinely new theorem boundary. Direct-reading evidence is still missing for the most dangerous baseline: the locally cached Pentus source resolves only to portal metadata rather than readable theorem text, so novelty against the strongest recognition baseline is not established. That prevents a pass. I do not make this a terminal reject because there is a concrete salvage path: upgrade the result to an explicit canonical fold/computation theorem with exact quantitative bounds and a primary-source comparison.
entries.strongest_objection: This looks like a Lean-internal repackaging of standard finite backward proof search: once Lambek derivability already has a cut-free decision procedure and the processing literature already treats elimination of rule-order ambiguity via focusing, chart methods, or proof nets, recasting acceptance on the reachable residual graph as a least fixed point with bounded saturation may be only a presentation-level variant, not a theorem-level advance.
entries.salvage_plan: Strengthen the result from an operator packaging to a sharper computational theorem: prove correctness for any reverse-topological enumeration, identify each intermediate fold state with the least predecessor-closed subset of the unprocessed region, derive exact convergence by graph distance, and then compare that package directly against primary focused-proof-search, chart-parsing, and Pentus-style recognition sources. If the contribution can be framed as a canonical executable semantics with quantitative bounds and proof-assistant-certified order independence, it may become a plausible note.

entries.statement: ∀ p : AtomicResidualState, ∀ L : List AtomicResidualState, L.Nodup → (∀ q : AtomicResidualState, q ∈ L ↔ Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep p q) → (∀ q r : AtomicResidualState, q ∈ L → r ∈ L → AtomicResidualGraphStep q r → List.idxOf q L < List.idxOf r L) → let good : Finset AtomicResidualState := List.foldr (fun q S => if q = ([# (p.2)], p.2) ∨ ∃ r : AtomicResidualState, AtomicResidualGraphStep q r ∧ r ∈ S then Finset.insert q S else S) (∅ : Finset AtomicResidualState) L; ∀ q : AtomicResidualState, q ∈ good ↔ q ∈ L ∧ AtomicResidualGraphAccepts q
entries.rationale: This is the strongest summary-level next theorem supported by the current verified core: acceptance is already exact on the finite reachable residual DAG, and the remaining bottleneck is to turn the abstract unique-labeling and least-subset facts into a concrete, presentation-independent computation. Compared with the rejected fixed-point packaging, this is a sharper canonical fold theorem rather than another semantic rebundling.
entries.reason: The candidate does not currently clear the paper-unit bar. On the present evidence, its delta over known Lambek decision-procedure and canonicalized-search ideas is mainly computational packaging, and even internally it appears downstream of your existing exact-acceptance and topological-characterization results rather than a new boundary theorem. The most dangerous baseline is not excluded by direct-reading primary evidence, so the novelty claim remains too weak for `pass`; however, there is a concrete salvage path if the fold theorem is expanded into a larger executable-and-quantitative package with stronger literature anchoring.
entries.strongest_objection: This looks like a corollary-level algorithmization of results you already have: once acceptance is already exact on the finite reachable residual DAG and already characterized via least predecessor-closed subsets and topological-order facts, replacing that package by a concrete reverse-topological `foldr` reads as an implementation theorem, not a new theorem-level boundary. The literature comparison also relies mainly on encyclopedia-level anchors and does not directly rule out prior Lambek parsing or proof-search papers already containing an equivalent dynamic-programming view.
entries.salvage_plan: Repackage the result as a stronger executable theory of the exact reachable residual DAG: prove the fold theorem together with the suffix invariant, derive exact path-length or saturation-round convergence bounds, state an order-independence/canonicality consequence, and support novelty with direct reading of primary Lambek parsing, focused proof search, and chart/proof-net sources rather than SEP summaries. Without those additions, the current statement remains too close to an internal computation corollary.

entries.statement: ∀ p : AtomicResidualState, ∀ L : List AtomicResidualState, L.Nodup → (∀ q : AtomicResidualState, q ∈ L ↔ Relation.ReflTransGen AtomicResidualGraphStep p q) → (∀ q r : AtomicResidualState, q ∈ L → r ∈ L → AtomicResidualGraphStep q r → List.idxOf q L < List.idxOf r L) → let goodStep : AtomicResidualState → Finset AtomicResidualState → Finset AtomicResidualState := fun q S => if q = ([# (p.2)], p.2) ∨ ∃ r : AtomicResidualState, r ∈ S ∧ AtomicResidualGraphStep q r then Finset.insert q S else S; ∀ i : Nat, ∀ q : AtomicResidualState, q ∈ List.foldr goodStep (∅ : Finset AtomicResidualState) (L.drop i) ↔ q ∈ L.drop i ∧ AtomicResidualGraphAccepts q
entries.rationale: This is the strongest summary-level next theorem supported by the verified core while avoiding the two rejected anti-targets. The theory already has exact acceptance, finiteness, least predecessor-closed characterization, unique Boolean labeling, and topological-order characterizations on the exact reachable DAG; the missing summary bridge is an explicit arbitrary-order fold theorem with a reusable intermediate invariant. This candidate turns those semantic and canonical results into one executable invariant, so the existing least-set, labeling, and terminal-sweep theorems become supporting views of a single reverse-sweep semantics.
entries.reason: The current candidate does not clear a strict paper-unit bar. Its delta over the verified core appears minor: it reorganizes already-established exact acceptance, least-set, labeling, and topological-sweep facts into a stronger intermediate fold invariant, but that still plausibly reads as a technical lemma or local continuation rather than an independently publishable theorem. The strongest novelty risk is unresolved because the closest baseline comparison relies mainly on survey/report material, and direct-reading evidence is still missing for the most dangerous baseline at the theorem level.
entries.strongest_objection: This looks like an implementation-level invariant inside an already-settled exact-reachable DAG theory, not a theorem-level advance: once acceptance is already characterized by least predecessor-closed subsets, unique labeling, and reverse-topological terminal sweeps, the suffix-wise `foldr` exactness theorem reads as a routine strengthening of existing machinery. The literature positioning is also too weak, because the closest baseline is only survey-level and no directly read primary source is shown to stop at the weaker boundary.
entries.salvage_plan: Strengthen the result into a broader algorithmic theorem family. Pair the suffix invariant with exact path-length or convergence-round bounds, derive a canonical dynamic-programming procedure with nontrivial quantitative consequences, and support novelty with direct reading of a dangerous primary baseline on Lambek focusing, proof nets, or chart parsing. Without that package, the claim remains too close to a corollary of the existing exact-reachable-region theory.
